ESPN UBA BASKETBALL NEWS
UBA Showcase Power Rankings

The Elite 8: Who's Truly the GOAT?

There can only be one champion, and The Council just leveled the playing field. Ruling that a league where all the top players stood 7'7" was killing the competitive spirit of the game, they reset everyone back to their original size. In my opinion, the old height boost let some undeserving players punch way above their actual talent level (cough, cough, Ndya). That leaves a power vacuum, and a wide-open runway for whoever's ready to be the face of a new era. Today, think of me as your TSA agent as we pull down some zippers and check these players' bags... and not in the Lil Dish way. Let's see who's actually got something to declare.
By Dix Insider
Jul 3, 2026
1

Alexander Smith

'07-'08 Houston Rockets • Point Guard
99OVR
The Breakdown: Let's get this out of the way. Most casuals would say that Ndya is the GOAT, but I'm not most casuals. In my book, it's Xander. He doesn't need to flop or pass or double team to win, he just needs the ball, a sliver of space, and about half a second. He gets to his spot at all three levels whenever he wants, and once he's got it, the shot's already good before it leaves his hand. Ndya has always game planned around ducking that 1v1, and honestly, smart move. Though he's no longer 7'7", this multi-xanillionaire still has unlimited range and boasts the best handle of all time. He's still utterly untouchable, height reset or not. In recent interviews, he's expressed his excitement for the competition, but feels his legacy already transcends this tournament, and after everything I've watched this man do for us, for basketball itself, I completely agree with him. And he's not a slasher! He has the deepest bag of all time. You idiot Ndya-tards need to stop leaving that nonsense in my comment sections!
2

Captain Jai-Ken

'85-'86 Boston Celtics • Point Guard
99OVR
The Breakdown: There was a time when Captain Jai-Ken never missed. Not as in statistically rare, as in literally never. It's a stat that should have been a red flag, but this was humanity's first contact with alien life, so they assumed it must be a level of skill humans just can't reach. Turns out the legendary floor general of the Space Ballers wasn't basketball perfection. He was rewriting the outcome in real time with the Slidey-Dex, an artifact so dangerous The Council destroyed it decades ago rather than risk it falling into the wrong hands again. The power got to him. Psychosis set in, he turned on his own teammates, and once the cheat was exposed, his career ended in disgrace. He tried to make several comebacks but was a complete shell of himself. That could have been the whole story. But, following the Council's decision on the power reset, Earth received an unexpected transmission from their old hero. Jai Ken looked a hundred years younger, happy, and exuded confidence, just like the captain of old. He said after meditating with Ndya in the Mountains, he realized the only way to make amends is to reunite the Space Ballers and bring them all the way to the Showcase final. He wants everyone to know he could care less about being the GOAT, he just wants his old friends back. All things considered, if he returns and plays like the Jai-Ken of old, I'm not sure anyone can stop him.
3

Anthony Smith III

'12-'13 Memphis Grizzlies • Shooting Guard
98OVR
The Breakdown: There's a strong case that AS3 is the best player never to touch 7'7", full stop. His recent run with the Hornets settled that argument as far as I'm concerned. His jumper is automatic from every level, and when he decides to put the ball on the floor instead, he's just as lethal driving to the rim. His defense is every bit as intimidating as his offense, the kind of two-way force that makes a possession feel over before it starts. My one issue with AS3 is the company he keeps. He's been running with Andy Reeves since before any of us knew the guy, and regular readers know exactly how I feel about that friendship. Still, I can separate the game from the grudge. Anthony's earned every bit of this ranking on his own. And look, I feel very confident in Xander's chances, but lately I've been waking up in cold sweats, the same two words on loop: Daddy's home.
4

Kiyan Anthony

'15-'16 Cleveland Cavaliers • Center
99OVR
The Breakdown: I consider Kiyan the perfect running mate for Xander (fingers crossed). Now, I did say running mate, not equal, don't get it twisted. Still, credit where it's due, the man averaged a wild 50 and 15 as a rookie. He's another name walking away from the 7'7" era, but unlike most of that crop, he was already dusting legends during his Master's College run before the height boost ever kicked in. Now that T-Lo isn't holding him back anymore, there's a real case Kiyan can become the second best player ever. He's got the frame and instincts of a traditional five, but he can shot-create like he's running the point. That combination shouldn't exist, but it does.
5

Andy Reeves

'12-'13 Memphis Grizzlies • Small Forward
97OVR
The Breakdown: Let's not pretend everyone doesn't already know where this is going. I've made my feelings about Ndya's reign clear for the past 10 years, and I'm not about to soften up just because he's back to a size that fits through a doorway. Strip away the height and what's left is honestly just a poor man's Skite Kalaitzakis. He's finally come down from "the Mountains" (i.e. Ydna's basement) to steal touches from his "best friend" Anthony Smith III. Right, because best friends leave one another behind when it's time for a height boost to save the universe. I do not respect the man. But all hate aside, I must admit he's still one of the best players in this tournament. He's not Ndya anymore, but there's still no one more efficient and crafty as this guy. He's deadly with the spin move and his IQ is off the charts (which he uses to exploit the rulebook at will). But hopefully, the refs can give other players a fair chance at being GOAT this time around.
6

Michael Allen

'10-'11 Chicago Bulls • Point Guard
99OVR
The Breakdown: There's no polite way to say this, so I won't try. Michael Allen is a walking eviction notice for opposing point guards. This shifty, downhill, rim reaper is the definition of unguardable. Get too tight on the perimeter and he's blown past you. Sag off and he's pulling from a zip code your defense doesn't cover. But the real story with Michael isn't the handle, it's the comeback. Longtime readers already know about his chicken addiction, and yes, it eventually caught up with him. He passed away from an overdose before The Council stepped in and brought him back, as referenced in the iconic tribute video that still gets played more than "Yung Barley".
7

Baba Maiga-Ba

'07-'08 Boston Celtics • Center
94OVR
The Breakdown: He's every hardcore fan and analyst's wet dream. At 7'3" he's the tallest name on this list, but he doesn't move like it. BMB's elite in the paint, comfortable in the midrange, and surprisingly live from three. As if that's not enough, he's also the best defender on this list, as no one else can match his ability to guard 1-5 with ease. The Council released a statement announcing Baba's move from small forward to the center position to meet height eligibility requirements, so it remains to see if he has what it takes to survive in the UBA's most competitive role. There's not a lot of BMB fans out there, but his performances will earn headlines instantly. Jump on the bandwagon while you can, Baba's stock is about to be higher than the XanCoin™.
8

Skite Kalaitzakis

'13-'14 Los Angeles Clippers • Small Forward
96OVR
The Breakdown: For years, the official record on what's known as the Consideration Circle said Skite Kalaitzakis' career flatlined the night Tony Wroten broke his ankles so badly he never wanted to play basketball again, and spent an entire half standing in shock at half court while the Space Ballers played four on five around him. That's the story everyone accepted. But it was recently revealed that's not what really happened. What actually happened is Captain Jai-Ken, tired of hearing Skite ask for more than his allotted one shot a game, used the Slidey-Dex to humiliate him in front of the entire universe, live, on purpose, for a laugh. Skite wasn't frozen out of embarrassment, he was so overcome with anger and heartbreak that he didn't even feel like living anymore. So Skite did the only thing that made sense to him. He stole the artifact, crossed the galaxy, and handed both the Slidey-Dex and the truth straight to The Council. That defection ended a friendship and dissolved the Space Ballers. His recent solo tear through UBA Street has people quietly wondering if the wrong Space Baller has been getting the flowers this whole time. I'll say what nobody else will: Skite never needed sliders.
The Road Ahead

Here's What The Bracket Reveal Looks Like

Eight players is nice. Sixteen teams settling it on the floor is better. This is the road to the Showcase final, laid bare, no excuses left for anybody. Tune in tonight to see it all play out. One night, one champion.
